justification: Ch3 inventory [22] covers John the Baptist as priestly co-heir with Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah (1QS IX:11, Damascus Document), but does not use Tabor's specific framing of the Qumran two-messiahs as a 'dual-kingship paradigm consistent with Hellenistic royal institutional models.' The chapter mentions the DSS dual messiah concept but does not explicitly draw the connection to Hellenistic dual-kingship models. The argument (John as priestly co-heir in a dual structure) exists; this specific Hellenistic-institutional framing of the Qumran evidence is new evidence for it.

John's universal kingdom message ('light to the nations') is about the nature of the early Christian movement as institutional and expansive. Ch5 inventory [1] addresses 'panta ta ethne' and the universalist scope of the movement as Greek imperial continuation. Ch5 inventory [0] frames the movement as continuation of Greek imperial tradition. This finding about John's universalist teaching belongs in Ch5 where the institutional-expansive mission framework is developed.

Ch3 inventory [42] 'Kingdom of God as Crown of God' explicitly presents the earthly-political reading: 'Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as in heaven' (Matt 6:10) and Luke 17:21. The finding's core claim—kingdom as earthly political reality focused on justice, not heavenly escape—is the same argument with the same Lord's Prayer evidence already in the chapter. Inventory [41] also covers basileia as political term for Hellenistic divine monarchies.

Ch3 inventory [25] argues 'Son of God' was the standard title for Hellenistic rulers, and inventory [6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture. The chapter does not use Tabor's specific formulation that anointing makes one 'Yahweh on earth'—this is a specific ancient Jewish mechanism (spirit-anointing conferring quasi-divine status) that strengthens the existing argument about Son of God as political-royal title. The concept that anointing confers divine representative status through spirit possession is not in the inventory.

Ch3 inventory [6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture at annunciation, baptism, and resurrection (Acts 13:33). Ch3 inventory [20] cites Acts 2:36 (God made Jesus both Kyrios and Christ). However, the specific exegesis of Romans 1:4—'designated Son of God in power according to the spirit of Holiness by his resurrection'—is not cited in the inventory. Romans 1:4 is a distinct Pauline text that marks resurrection as the moment of designation, adding new evidence (specific verse) to the existing argument about resurrection as political legitimation.

Ch3 inventory [20] explicitly covers 'one God the Father... one Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Cor 8:5-6) and Phil 2:11 ('Jesus Christ is Kyrios, to the glory of God the Father'). The contrast between pagan lords/Caesar and Paul's one Lord Jesus Christ is the core of the Father vs. Kyrios distinction developed in the 'Acts and the Epistles' section (lines 324-331). The finding's specific evidence (1 Cor 8:5-6 contrast of many lords with one Lord) matches inventory [20] exactly.

Ch3 inventory [26] covers 'only-begotten of the Father' as exclusive succession language, and inventory [50] discusses Mary as dynastic mother. However, Tabor's specific 'two-Adam christology'—Jesus as 'last Adam' with followers becoming 'sons of God' as 'firstborn of many brothers and sisters'—is a distinct Pauline framework (Romans 8:29) not cited in ch3's inventory. The argument that kingdom titles function as political succession language exists; this Adamic-dynastic framing from Paul is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [20] explicitly cites Phil 2:11 ('Jesus Christ is Kyrios, to the glory of God the Father') in the Acts and Epistles section (lines 324-331). The Philippians 2 exaltation hymn—God highly exalting Jesus and giving him the name above every name so every knee should bow—is the same passage. The argument about imperial honor and authority terminology conferring political-theological legitimacy is the chapter's core thesis about kyrios as royal title (inventory [32]).

Ch3 inventory [41] explicitly states: 'Basileia etymologically from basileus = kingship/reign/sovereignty, not primarily territory' and 'LSJ and BDAG: reign, rule, sovereignty as primary meaning.' This is exactly what Tabor says: 'the word Kingdom in both Hebrew and Greek means government or rule.' The chapter also argues it was understood as political revolution (inventory [42], [45], [46]). Same argument, same evidence.

Ch3 inventory [46] argues 'Jesus's call to repent and believe (Mark 1:15) was a summons to national realignment under Roman occupation, not a call to private moral reform.' Inventory [42] covers earthly restoration of sovereignty. Inventory [47] discusses family betrayal as political language with 'machaira = soldiers weapon.' Tabor's characterization of Jesus as 'a political revolutionary who expected nothing less than the violent overthrow of the kingdoms of the world' is the same argument already made across inventories [41]-[49].

Ch3 inventory [5] mentions Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to sons of Zeus (Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus) and inventory [25] covers Son of God as Hellenistic royal title. However, Tabor's specific discussion of humans becoming 'star beings' through apotheosis—with the explicit acknowledgment 'Paul here is plugging into a whole set of Hellenistic concepts'—adds the specific mechanism of human-to-divine transformation (apotheosis/divinization) as a Hellenistic pattern. While the chapter discusses divine titles, it does not specifically discuss the process of apotheosis/divinization as a Greco-Mediterranean pattern of humans becoming divine. This is new evidence for the existing argument.

Ch3 inventory [2] covers Jesus's titles as pre-existing political terms, and inventory [5] cites Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to sons of Zeus. The chapter acknowledges Greek divine parallels. However, Mark's specific engagement with the Hellenistic 'divine man' (theios aner) concept through Apollonius of Tyana—and Mark's deliberate rejection of miracle-working as the basis for messiahship while still operating within that framework—is not in the inventory. This is a specific literary-rhetorical strategy in Mark showing awareness of and negotiation with the divine-man category.

Ch3 inventory [25] argues Son of God was the standard Hellenistic ruler title from Egyptian Son of Ra, and inventory [5] covers Justin Martyr's comparison to sons of Zeus. However, the specific 4th-5th century BCE Greek lamella with 'I am the son of Earth and star-filled Heaven' as a salvation confession—with 'lordship among the other Heroes' and 'heaven alone is my home'—is a specific archaeological artifact predating Christianity by centuries that is not cited in the inventory. This provides concrete pre-Christian Greek evidence for the Son of God/Heaven language with ascension and lordship claims.

Ch3 inventory [22] cites Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah (1QS IX:11, Damascus Document) and inventory [4] cites 4Q246. However, the Teacher of Righteousness claiming 'I have been exalted to the Council of the Elohim, I have a seat among the gods'—and Tabor's explicit connection to Hellenistic patterns of suffering → descent → victory → divine exaltation—is not in the inventory. The specific DSS text about the teacher's divine exaltation, and the link to Hellenistic divine-man suffering-and-exaltation patterns, is new evidence for the existing argument about Son of God as political-theological title conferred through a process.

Ch3 inventory [41]-[43] argue Kingdom of God was political institution with basileia as Hellenistic term. However, the specific structural evidence—James as head with council of 12 on thrones and council of 70 elders forming a literal government—is not cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument exists; this specific governmental hierarchy evidence is new.

Ch3 inventory [32] covers kyrios as imperial honorific. However, Tabor's specific point about the Greek word 'thronos' being deliberately used (versus other words for chair/seat) to designate Simon's succession as an authority position—this specific terminological evidence about thronos in succession contexts is not in the inventory. The argument about Hellenistic royal terminology exists; the specific thronos terminological point is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [5] cites Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to Heracles and Asclepius, and the existing argument connects Jesus to Greek divine categories. However, the specific framing of resurrection as Greek apotheosis—'leaving the shell behind, leaving the old clothes behind, your essential self which is now naked'—and its explicit naming as apotheosis rather than Jewish resurrection categories is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument (Jesus understood through Greek categories) exists; the specific apotheosis framing of the resurrection is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [42] argues the NT evidence 'overwhelmingly points to earthly restoration of sovereignty, not an abstract heaven or inner spirituality.' Inventory [46] argues Jesus's call was 'a summons to national realignment under Roman occupation, not a call to private moral reform.' The finding—that early Christian theology transformed the original earthly political Kingdom into a heavenly spiritual concept—is the same argument. The chapter explicitly argues for the original political meaning and against the later spiritualization.

Ch3 inventory [50] discusses Mary as dynastic mother with Ptolemaic/Seleucid queen titles. Ch2 inventory [37]-[38] cover Mary's royal descent and Hasmonean connection. However, Tabor's specific framing of Mary as having 'double royalty'—combined priestly and royal lineage—is not specifically cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument about Mary's dynastic significance exists; the specific 'double royalty' (priestly + kingly) framing is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [41] covers basileia tou theou as technical term for Hellenistic divine monarchies. Inventory [42] cites 'Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as in heaven' (Matt 6:10). The finding presents 'rule of God' breaking into Satan's political dominion using Lord's Prayer evidence—the same argument and same evidence. The chapter's Pater Noster section (inventory [12]) covers the Lord's Prayer in detail including kingdom descending from heaven.

Ch3 inventory [22] covers John the Baptist as priestly co-heir with baptism as investiture, citing 'Baptism of Jesus as priestly initiation of royal messiah per Qumran rule 1QSa 2:11-22.' However, the specific archaeological evidence from the Suba Cave—anointing of the right foot with oil preceding baptism—is not cited. This is concrete archaeological evidence for baptism as royal anointing/installation that strengthens the existing argument about baptism as investiture.

Ch3 inventory [14] in 'Why This Imagery Was Still Alive in the First Century' explicitly cites 'Early Christian art: Christ as Helios; Sunday as holy day.' The finding about Jesus portrayed as Sol Invictus in a ceiling mosaic beneath the Vatican is the same evidence. Inventory [18] discusses the halo as solar disk. The argument that Jesus was understood through Greek divine-royal solar imagery and not exclusively Jewish categories is thoroughly covered.

Ch3 inventory [42] covers earthly restoration of sovereignty. Ch5 inventory [43] covers Paul employing Greek cosmology (Platonic-Stoic metaphysics). However, Tabor's specific identification of Paul's cosmology as 'platonic dualistic' ('this world is not my home, I'm just passing through') is new evidence about how Paul grounded eschatology in Greco-Roman philosophical frameworks. The argument about Greek philosophical categories shaping early Christianity exists (inventory [9], [11]); Paul's explicit Platonic dualism is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [42] covers the earthly-political reading of the kingdom: 'The NT evidence overwhelmingly points to earthly restoration of sovereignty.' Inventory [46] argues Jesus's call was 'a summons to national realignment under Roman occupation.' The finding cites Paula Richardson and 'Jesus had a program of dismantling Rome,' which adds a scholarly name but the argument—kingdom as political-social reality rather than purely apocalyptic—is the same argument the chapter makes. The Q&A global section on 'Apocalyptic language = rebellion language' also addresses this.

Ch3 inventory [5] cites Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to sons of Zeus, and the chapter argues for understanding Jesus through Greek categories. However, Tabor's specific argument citing John Granger Cook's documentation of apotheosis parallels across the ancient world—framing earliest resurrection understanding as Greco-Roman apotheosis (spiritual transformation and elevation) rather than corpse resuscitation—adds specific scholarly evidence and the explicit apotheosis-as-earliest-resurrection claim. The argument that Jesus was understood through Hellenistic categories exists; Cook's apotheosis documentation is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [24] discusses Messiah as authentic Judean royal title. Ch4 inventory [21] covers 'The baptism of Jesus is a coronation ceremony, not a repentance ritual.' However, Tabor's specific argument linking Mark's Palm Sunday narrative to 1 Kings 1—Solomon brought to Gihon, anointed, proclaimed king—and interpreting the triumphal entry as a deliberate reenactment of Solomon's royal succession at the same geography is not in the ch3 inventory. This is a specific OT parallel (1 Kings 1) that the chapter does not cite.

Ch3 inventory [14] discusses how Egyptian solar-royal culture was alive in the 1st century and mentions 'Imperial posture transfer: Jesus at well using Prima Porta Augustus stance.' Ch2 inventory [19]-[20] cover Hellenization of Galilee. However, Tabor's specific point about the Temple Mount as 'the largest temple and compound in all of the ancient world, nothing in Rome rivals this' designed in Greco-Roman architectural style is not cited in ch3. The argument about Hellenistic cultural continuity exists; Herod's Temple as supreme example of Greco-Roman style is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [23] mentions 'Early Jerusalem ossuaries with Iesous in Greek + ichthus symbol.' However, the specific Talpiot tomb Jonah inscription mixing Greek ('dios', 'raise up') with Hebrew ('Yahweh', 'hgba') is distinct archaeological evidence not cited in the ch3 inventory. This Greek-Hebrew syncretism in resurrection theology from a tomb near the Jesus family burial site is new evidence for the existing argument about Greco-Christian synthesis.

Ch3 inventory [2] argues titles were pre-existing political terms and inventory [25] covers Son of God as Hellenistic ruler title. However, the specific point that Jesus's 'who do men say that I am?' declaration occurred at Caesarea Philippi—an explicitly Hellenistic city with temples to Pan and Augustus—thus situating the Son of God claim in a Hellenistic institutional context, is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument exists; the specific geographic-institutional context of Caesarea Philippi is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [42] covers earthly-political reading ('the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord'). However, the specific detail that disciples immediately discussed political succession ('who's going to be the greatest, who's going to be on the right and left hand, who's going to rule and reign') after Jesus proclaimed the kingdom 'with power' in the Hellenistic setting of Caesarea Philippi is not specifically cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument exists; the succession-discussion context is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [41]-[42] argue Kingdom of God as political institution. Inventory [43] covers apostoloi as royal emissaries. However, Tabor's specific point about the Essene/Qumran community self-understanding as replacement for the Temple ('we are the temple') is not cited in ch3 despite the chapter using DSS evidence elsewhere (inventory [4], [22]). This is a concrete example of a Jewish group reconceiving political-theological institutions as a body of people rather than a building, directly paralleling the book's kingdom argument.

Ch3 inventory [14] cites 'Early Christian art: Christ as Helios; Sunday as holy day' and inventory [16] covers Sunday reflecting the Ptolemaic planetary week. However, the specific evidence from synagogue mosaics showing Jesus as a 'divine solar being' announcing a new cosmic age (Pisces)—with both Christians and Jews sharing this zodiac iconography—is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about solar divine imagery exists; zodiac mosaic evidence with Piscean age symbolism is new.

Ch3 inventory [9] discusses God the Father in Greek philosophical categories (Plato, Stoics) and inventory [14] covers Egyptian solar-royal culture alive in the 1st century. However, the specific claim that earliest Jewish Christians 'absorbed rich astrological understandings from Egyptian hermeticism' and incorporated Alexandrian scientific and philosophical advances from the beginning is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about Greek-Egyptian intellectual frameworks exists; Egyptian Hermetic absorption by earliest Christians is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [14] in 'Why This Imagery Was Still Alive in the First Century' explicitly cites 'Early Christian art: Christ as Helios' and 'Christ flanked by stars mirroring Hadrian's mausoleum Helios imagery.' The finding about early Christian chapels beneath the Vatican portraying Jesus as Sol Invictus is the same evidence already cited—Vatican Sol Invictus mosaic is the most famous example of Christ-as-Helios art.

Ch3 inventory [41]-[48] argue Kingdom of God as political institution. However, the specific Essene 'we are the temple' self-understanding, housed in the wilderness where God's spirit dwells—functioning as a political-theological institution (body of men replacing physical temple)—is the same finding as uBxXz69Tncc_0 and is not cited in ch3. The argument about kingdom as political institution exists; the Qumran community's 'temple of men' concept is new evidence. Note: this is essentially the same finding as uBxXz69Tncc_0.

Ch3 inventory [9] discusses God the Father in Greek philosophical categories (Plato, Stoics) and inventory [11] covers the Greek Platonic model of the immortal soul. However, Tabor's specific point about Paul adopting 'explicit Platonic dualistic cosmology' with 'this world is not my home' framing is new evidence about how early Christian eschatology was grounded in Greco-Roman philosophy. This is essentially the same finding as mJfeR5CtZ1M_1. The argument exists; Paul's explicit Platonic dualism is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [5] cites Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to sons of Zeus (Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus). However, Tabor's detailed biographical parallels with Apollonius of Tyana—supernatural birth, itinerant preaching, disciples, miracles, post-mortem appearances—constitute a more detailed and specific parallel than Justin's brief comparison. Apollonius is not cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument about Greek divine parallels exists; the specific Apollonius biographical parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [5] cites Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to Heracles and others. However, Tabor's specific comparison to Romulus—comparing Jesus's post-resurrection apotheosis (seated at right hand of God) to the divinization of Romulus and other Greek heroes—adds a specific Roman parallel (Romulus) not cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument about understanding Jesus through Greek apotheosis categories exists; the Romulus parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [25] argues 'Son of God' was the standard Hellenistic ruler title, and ch2 inventory [31]-[35] extensively cover Davidic genealogical claims. Tabor's point that Paul testifies to Jesus's Davidic lineage ('seat of David') as evidence of a royal status claim is covered by the book's treatment of genealogies as dynastic instruments (ch2 inventory [31]: 'Formal genealogies in the Gospels signal a dynastic claim'). The argument and evidence (Pauline Davidic testimony) are present.

Ch3 inventory [24] discusses Messiah/Christos as authentic Judean royal title. However, Tabor's argument that 'Christ'/Anointed in Revelation is a distinct theological concept from Jesus interpolations—visible in 'the kingdoms of this world have become the kingdoms of our Lord and of his Christ' where 'and of his Christ' appears as a separate layer—is a new argument about textual stratigraphy. Ch3 inventory [42] actually cites Rev 11:15 as evidence, but the ARGUMENT drawn from it is different: ch3 uses it to show earthly restoration; Tabor uses it to argue Christ/Anointed had roots in royal anointing tradition separate from Jesus cult. Same evidence, different argument.

Ch3 inventory [10] argues Gnostics and Marcionites denied Father was YHWH, and inventory [9] discusses the identification of God the Father as not fixed. However, Tabor's specific point about the fundamental monotheistic theology in Revelation's original text and the theological 'strain' in reconciling Jesus worship with Jewish monotheism—with this tension being absent in the Gospels—is a new textual observation. The argument about Hellenistic frameworks being necessary to integrate Jesus into monotheism exists; Revelation's specific monotheistic strain is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture formula, and inventory [22] mentions priestly and royal merged authority. However, Tabor's specific identification of Psalm 110 as 'quoted more in the New Testament than any other text' regarding the Messianic hope, with its combined king-and-priest figure ('you are a priest forever according to my word, o my righteous King'), is not cited in ch3. While ch3 inventory [19] cites Ps 110:1 for the 'two Lords' text, it uses it for kyrios analysis, not for the combined priest-king messianic argument. The priest-king Psalm 110 evidence is new for the combined-authority argument.

Ch3 inventory [19] cites Ps 110:1 / Mark 12:36 ('Eipen Kyrios to Kyrio mou'). Ch3 inventory [24] covers Messiah as Judean royal title. However, Tabor's specific argument—that Jesus questioned whether 'the Messiah is a son of David' and quoted Psalm 110 to reconceptualize Messianic identity beyond traditional Davidic descent—is a new argument. The chapter uses Ps 110 for kyrios analysis, but does not present Jesus's own questioning of Davidic messianic identity as evidence of reconceptualization. The evidence (Ps 110, Mark 12:35-37) appears in the book; the argument drawn from it is new.

Ch3 inventory [52] argues 'Son of God' was a political title, not a supernatural claim. Ch5 inventory [23] discusses Paul's silence on Jesus's daily sayings. However, Tabor's specific distinction between the 'Gospel of Jesus' (kingdom message about God's mercy and renewed Torah) versus the later 'Gospel about Jesus' (Paul's message about divinity and salvation)—showing how titles shifted from political to theological meaning—is a specific framework not cited in the ch3 inventory. The argument about the political-to-theological shift exists; this specific Gospel-of-Jesus vs. Gospel-about-Jesus framing is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [52] argues Son of God was political not supernatural. Inventory [10] mentions Gnostics and Marcionites denying YHWH identification. However, the specific Ebionite evidence—Jewish Christians who believed Jesus was Messiah and prophet but 'not God or Divine in any sense,' eventually 'overwhelmed by Gentile Christianity'—is not cited in the ch3 inventory. The Ebionites provide direct historical evidence that early Jewish Christians did not interpret Son of God as divine nature, which is new evidence for the existing political-title argument.

Ch3 inventory [25] argues Son of God was Hellenistic ruler title derived from Son of Ra. However, Tabor's specific framing as 'high human christology'—where a human being becomes deified through exaltation rather than being a pre-existent divine being—articulates a specific mechanism (human deification through exaltation) not in the ch3 inventory. The argument that Son of God is political-royal exists; the 'high human christology' (human deified through process) is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [20] explicitly covers 1 Cor 8:5-6 ('one God the Father... one Lord Jesus Christ') and Phil 2:11. Inventory [19] distinguishes Father-language from Kyrios-language in Jesus's speech. Inventory [32] covers kyrios as imperial honorific. Tabor's distinction between kyrios (Lord/Master = position of authority) and theos (God the Father)—with Jesus 'not God the Father and he never will be'—is the same Father vs. Kyrios distinction the chapter makes in the 'Acts and the Epistles' section using the same evidence (1 Cor 8:5-6).

Ch3 inventory [26] covers 'Son of Father' (Philopator) as Hellenistic title and cites Plutarch on Plato's conception involving dynamis and pneuma. However, Tabor's specific 'second Adam christology' from Philippians 2 and Genesis 1:26—both first Adam and Jesus are 'in the form of God,' but Jesus 'did not grasp at equality' and instead 'took the form of a Slave,' leading to exaltation—is a specific Pauline framework not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about royal/divine status through Hellenistic categories exists; the specific Adam-Christ Philippians 2 framework is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [41]-[43] argue Kingdom of God as political institution with 12 thrones (inventory [42]). However, Tabor's specific parallel between the Council of 12 with 3 pillars (James, Peter, John) and the Qumran Community Rule ('12 men and three priests')—showing the same organizational structure—is not cited in ch3. While inventory [22] cites Qumran rules for dual messiah, this specific organizational parallel (12+3 structure) is new evidence for the kingdom-as-institution argument.

Ch3 inventory [25] explicitly argues 'Son of God was the standard title for Hellenistic rulers' with evidence including 'Alexander as son of God; Ptolemies as sons of God.' Inventory [2] lists Son of God among titles that 'were pre-existing political terms of Greek and Roman kingship' and cites Augustus as divi filius. Tabor's statement that 'in the Hellenistic world, emperors and divine men were called sons of god, the emperor is called a son of god, we have a dozen other figures called sons of god' is the same argument with the same evidence.

Ch3 inventory [25] covers 'Son of God as standard title for Hellenistic rulers' with Egyptian Son of Ra evidence. Inventory [6] covers Psalm 2:7 as royal investiture for Davidic kings. The argument that 'every anointed Davidic king is called son of God in a special way' is covered by inventory [6] (Psalm 2:7 as investiture formula) and inventory [24] (Messiah as authentic Judean royal title). Both the Jewish and Greco-Roman contexts of Son of God as royal-political title are present.

Ch3 inventory [25] covers Son of God as Hellenistic ruler title. However, Tabor's specific framing of 'firstborn of many' sons of God—where believers are transformed through resurrection into divine beings in a process (institutional-theological structure with membership and transformation)—is new evidence about the mechanism of kingdom membership. The argument about kingdom as institution exists; the 'process of creating many sons of God through resurrection' as institutional membership mechanism is not in the inventory.

Ch3 inventory [24] covers Messiah as royal title, and inventory [2] argues titles were pre-existing political terms. Ch4 inventory [21] discusses baptism as coronation ceremony. However, the specific reading of John's crucifixion narrative as a royal enthronement—Jesus titled 'King of the Jews' in three languages as inthronisation, death as exaltation and glorification—is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about royal-political framework for understanding Jesus exists; John's crucifixion-as-enthronement reading is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [5] explicitly cites Justin Martyr (First Apology 21-22) arguing Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus' including Heracles. Inventory [5] also cites Theophilus of Antioch on Heracles ascending after the pyre. The finding that Greek audiences would find Jesus's divinity claim less problematic because of figures like Heracles being elevated to divine status—the 'more permeable boundary between human and divine in Greco-Roman thought'—is the same argument with the same type of evidence.

Ch3 inventory [24] covers Messiah/Christos as royal title meaning 'anointed.' Ch4 inventory [21] discusses baptism as coronation ceremony. However, the specific German scholarship framing anointing as 'the most visually spectacular element' of the enthronement ritual, serving as pars pro toto for the entire enthronement process—anointing as the publicly visible symbol that named the whole ceremony—is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about anointing as royal-political ritual exists; this specific pars pro toto mechanism is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [2] lists 'Son of God' among titles that were 'pre-existing political terms of Greek and Roman kingship' and specifically cites 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals.' Inventory [25] covers 'Son of God was the standard title for Hellenistic rulers.' The finding identifies 'Son of God' (filius dei/gottes) as a Roman imperial title used for Octavian, Tiberius, Nero, Titus, and Domitian on coins—the same argument and type of evidence (imperial coinage with divine sonship titles). The chapter already makes this point with Augustus as divi filius.

Ch3 inventory [2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms. However, the specific claim that Mark's Gospel attributes the same miraculous deeds to Jesus that Roman historiography attributes to Vespasian—healing the blind with spittle, healing the withered hand—as anti-imperial propaganda is not in the ch3 inventory. While inventory [24] mentions Vespasian being identified with Jewish oracle, the specific miracle-reassignment argument (Vespasian's miracles reattributed to Jesus by Mark) is new evidence for the existing argument about Jesus's titles and deeds functioning as counter-imperial claims.

Ch3 inventory [32] covers kyrios as standard imperial honorific. However, the specific comparison of the Parousia (Christ's return) to Roman imperial ceremonies—'politische Inszenierung' (political staging) of emperor's arrival—is not in the ch3 inventory. Ch5 inventory does not cover Parousia either. The argument about Christian terminology using Hellenistic political vocabulary exists; the specific Parousia-as-imperial-arrival parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [40] discusses Jesus's birth as new star in Greek tradition (Alexander, Ptolemy III, Augustus). However, the specific evidence about Jupiter-Saturn conjunctions as indicators of messianic births across the Greco-Persian world—with Jupiter as 'king's star,' Saturn as 'star of the Jews,' and the conjunction in Pisces—is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about celestial signs confirming divine kingship exists; the specific Jupiter-Saturn conjunction mechanism from Hellenistic royal astrology is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [2] covers titles as pre-existing political terms across multiple empires. However, the specific structural parallel between Parthian Zoroastrian messianism and Jewish messianism—both expecting a divinely-chosen ruler from 'the apex of political power' to establish peace—is not in the ch3 inventory. The comparative imperial titulature argument exists; the Parthian-Zoroastrian parallel with Mithraic messianic expectations is new evidence. The chapter inventory notes this area as needing more evidence on comparative titulature.

Ch2 inventory [30] covers euangelion as 'political term for proclamation of royal victories or accessions.' However, the specific German scholarship explaining evangelium as Roman political terminology used for announcing events in the emperor's life (accession, birthday, military victories)—and Paul deliberately adopting this 'politically charged' term as a 'subversive' rival claim to Caesar—is not in the ch3 inventory. The argument exists in ch2; for ch3 specifically, this strengthens the existing argument about Christian terminology being political, but the evangelium-as-political-term evidence is not currently in ch3's inventory.

Ch3 inventory [2] explicitly cites 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and argues titles were 'pre-existing political terms of Greek and Roman kingship.' Inventory [25] covers Son of God as standard Hellenistic ruler title. The finding—that Roman emperors were designated 'Son of God' during their lifetimes and Mark's use of this title for Jesus operates as 'counter-imperial contrast'—is the same argument. Acts 17:6-7 (inventory [1]) demonstrates the political competition: 'another king, Jesus.'

Ch3 inventory [2] covers the anti-imperial dimension of Jesus's titles. However, the specific claim about recent German monographic studies analyzing 'political-military semantics' in Mark's gospel—reading Mark as an anti-imperial political text related to the Vespasian era and the First Jewish-Roman War (~70 CE)—is new evidence (recent scholarly trend) for the existing argument. The specific scholarly direction of political Markuslektüre is not in the inventory.

Ch2 inventory [55] covers 'Jesus was crucified by Rome for political claims of kingship, not for religious blasphemy; crucifixion was Rome's punishment for political threats.' However, the specific statistical evidence—crucifixion used in 95% of cases to suppress rebellion (Aufruhr) and maintain provincial control as a political punishment for maintaining wealth flow from provinces to Rome—is not in the ch3 inventory. For ch3's argument about kingdom language and resurrection as political legitimacy claims, the political nature of crucifixion as evidence is new within ch3 specifically.

Ch3 inventory [27] explicitly covers: 'The Logos concept was a central Greek philosophical theme developed by Heraclitus, Stoics, and Middle Platonists before Philo of Alexandria and John's Gospel.' The evidence includes 'Stoics: Zeno of Citium, Chrysippus — Logos as rational cosmic principle (Diogenes Laertius 7.134-147).' The finding about Stoic Logos being connected to Johannine Logos theology is the same argument and evidence already in the chapter.

Ch3 inventory [9] covers 'Paul and John describe the Father in Greek philosophical categories' with evidence including 'Gospel of John presents Jesus as Logos, term from Greek philosophy.' Inventory [27] covers Stoics and Logos. The finding about God communicating through Pneuma, Nous, and Logos as Stoic technical terms is covered by the chapter's existing treatment of Greek philosophical frameworks for articulating God's nature. Inventory [9] specifically cites the Areopagus speech using 'language of Plato and Stoics.'

Ch3 inventory [32] covers kyrios as standard imperial honorific ('Kyrios Kaisar'). Inventory [20] covers the Father vs. Kyrios distinction. However, the specific chronological competition—that simultaneously during Paul's lifetime, both the Roman Emperor (Claudius, Nero) and Jesus were claiming the title 'Kyrios,' creating a fundamental conflict over ultimate authority—is a specific historical-chronological framing not in the ch3 inventory. The argument about kyrios as imperial title exists; the specific temporal simultaneity of the competing claims is new evidence.

Ch3 inventory [2] argues titles were pre-existing political terms and cites Augustus as divi filius. Inventory [25] covers Son of God as standard Hellenistic title. However, the specific evidence from Asian provincial inscriptions—Augustus publicly proclaimed as divine savior (soter) with his birthday marked as 'beginning of good news' (evangelion)—and Paul's direct confrontation of this imperial theology by declaring Jesus 'Son of God from the house of David' rather than from the imperial dynasty, is not in the ch3 inventory with this specificity. The Asian provincial inscription evidence about Augustus's birthday as evangelion is new evidence for the existing argument.
